annotations.text: The olfactory hallucinations suggest a focal seizure arising in the uncus.
The mass in the brain may be neoplastic or nonneoplastic in origin.

Tumefactive, enhancing nonneoplastic brain lesions can be caused by inflammatory diseases, such as multiple sclerosis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis, sarcoidosis, Behçet’s disease with neurologic involvement, tuberculosis, fungal infections, and syphilitic gumma.
Given the presence of calcification and the prepontine lesion, which suggests meningeal involvement, a unifying diagnosis could be cerebral sarcoidosis.
The 1-year history of symptoms suggests that the process is indolent.
We were concerned that this patient could have a glial tumor, either low-grade or a higher grade, and so we prepared to perform a diagnostic and also potentially therapeutic procedure.
The absence of these findings shifted our clinical suspicion toward a diagnosis of a brain tumor.
The granulomas were mostly compact and well-formed with central collections of large histiocytes surrounded by a lymphocyte-rich zone with a mild degree of fibrosis.
tumor? Findings on imaging studies of the mass are nonspecific. The prepontine meningeal enhancing lesion is indicative of an inflammatory process and very much argues against a neoplasm.

Immunosuppressive drugs, predominantly glucocorticoids, are the backbone of therapy for neurosarcoidosis in all its forms, but biologics such as TNF-α inhibitors are now an important part of the treatment.

Case reports, small series, and our personal experiences have shown that infliximab has a benefit in refractory cases of neurosarcoidosis, often with dramatic resolution of brain and spinal-cord lesions and corresponding clinical improvement.
